Data in the science universe is pretty bad.

The portals are designed by, I think, grad students, maybe not scientists.

And what we had to do for that to work was scrape data from like all over.
And that actually that the team got a Pulitzer Prize for web scraping, which is quite interesting in and of itself.

So like Italy, for instance, did a good job.
They put all their numbers on GitHub.
It was really easy.
It was JSON, et cetera.
In the US, most of the time data was per county.
And those counties all had different systems.
So you had to have different scrapers per county at the US level.

One of my favorite ones was France, which I worked on a scraper for where like every day someone would get all the numbers from all of France, paste them on the JPEG with like a nice textured background and put the JPEG on the web such that scraping that involved a lot of processing to clean it up enough to OCR it to get the data out.
And so this is the kind of situation where you do need stuff.
And like more generally, even without COVID, you know, like if you imagine the impact of making research better, like every time we make it slightly better, scientists can science more.

Well, so in that blog post I referenced earlier, like you can think of like a number of properties that would be pretty cool to have in, you know, sort of like the scientific data space in terms of discovery of data repositories, data sets, et cetera.

And Volcker, um, has made a tool with the IPF foundation called Matadisco.
And what Matadisco does is extremely simple.
It tracks data that gets published to all kinds of different, um, data sets and data sources.
Currently it's limited to just a few because it's still like ramping up.

But the idea is to put all the metadata around all, everything that happens, everything that's published, um, directly on AT Proto, such that then you can write apps that just listen to, uh, whatever jet stream, whatever's, whatever's convenient in terms of fire hoses for you.
And then react to that and do things with that data, pull it down, et cetera, knowing of course that, um, all of this is nicely verified.
And so if you, if you think of the list of properties that I, I, I produced earlier, well, it hits quite a number of them, right?
You have something that's inherently social.
You can subscribe to it.
You have a right to exit because it's all on the PDS.
That's part of the protocol.
And, you know, sustainably fundable.
Well, it's not very expensive, um, but like we could, we could figure it out.

Um, and, and there's a few properties that it doesn't completely hit, but in general, like simply by pushing all this metadata about data sets to the, to the protocol is getting a lot of these things working already.
